questions.question: What is the 'Composure Principle' in sales discovery?

options.text: Always agreeing with the prospect so they feel comfortable.
Staying calm and maintaining your methodology when challenged, proving your professional conviction.
Hiding your emotions so the prospect can't read your true thoughts.
Admitting that the prospect is right even when they aren't.

questions.explanation: Your reaction to resistance tells the prospect whether you are a desperate vendor or a confident expert who believe in their solution.

questions.question: A prospect says, 'I've got 5 minutes. Get to the point.' What is the most effective response?

options.text: Panic and try to explain every feature of your product as fast as possible.
Apologize profusely and ask to reschedule for a better time.
Acknowledge the limit and ask one high-impact question about their biggest bottleneck to establish immediate value.
Tell them that 5 minutes is disrespectful to your expertise.

questions.explanation: Directly addressing the time constraint while pivoting to high-value discovery respects their time and shows you can perform under pressure.

questions.question: Why is the 'I May Not Be Right For You' reframe so powerful with skeptical prospects?

options.text: It tricks them into feeling sorry for you.
It gives you an excuse to end the call early and avoid hard questions.
It creates 'negative pull' by showing you aren't desperate for the deal, which paradoxically builds massive trust.
It makes the prospect feel like they aren't 'good enough' for your product.

questions.explanation: Willingness to walk away is a hallmark of an expert. It moves the conversation from 'selling' to 'diagnosing'.

questions.question: A prospect says, 'Your tool looks exactly like [Competitor].' How should you respond using the 'Acknowledge, Don't Argue' pattern?

options.text: 'No, we are 100% different. Let me show you 10 features they don't have.'
'That's fair—from the outside, we look similar. The difference usually shows up in [Specific Area]. What's most important to you when comparing tools?'
'[Competitor] is actually terrible and we have much better reviews.'
'If you like them so much, you should just buy their product.'

questions.explanation: Joining their side of the table and providing a logical technical distinction is far more effective than defensive arguing.

questions.question: In a simulation with a 'High D' (Dominant) resistant prospect, what is the best strategy?

options.text: Be fast-paced, goal-oriented, and don't take their challenges personally.
Try to talk about their personal life and hobbies to soften them up.
Wait for them to tell you what to do next.
Use lots of flowery language and adjectives to describe your product.

questions.explanation: High D personalities respect directness and competence. They communicate through challenge and value outcomes over relationships.

questions.question: Recall the last time a prospect pushed back or challenged you. Did you capitulate (start pitching), escalate (get defensive), or maintain your composure? Draft a 'neutralizing' response for that specific objection using the Acknowledge-Redirect pattern. How would following this pattern have changed the dynamic of that conversation?
questions.promptForAI: Evaluate the student's self-reflection. Confirm their drafted response correctly uses the Acknowledge-Redirect architecture (Agree with the validity of the thought, redirect to a diagnostic question). Suggest one follow-up 'Impact' question they could use to gain further leverage.
